text: I was wondering whether you could help me.
Could you tell me where the station is?
Do you know what time the tour starts?
I was wondering whether breakfast is included.
Would you mind telling me how this works?
Good morning! The coffee here is wonderful, isn't it? I hope you don't mind me asking, do you happen to know whether the museum is open today?
It is! And yes, it opens at ten. You're not from here either, are you? I was wondering where you were from.
I'm from Brazil, travelling alone for a few weeks. It's so easy to strike up a conversation here, everyone is lovely.
I know what you mean. I'm from Canada, here for work. Could you tell me how long you're staying? You might like the food market on Sundays.
Another week, so I'd love that. Would you mind telling me where exactly it is? I never know who to ask.
Just behind the cathedral. Honestly, it's been so nice chatting. We've really hit it off, isn't it funny how a question over coffee turns into a real conversation?

Sorry to bother you -- I was wondering if you could help me out. I'm new in town and a little lost. Do you happen to know whether the old market is open on a Sunday? And would you mind telling me which bus goes up to the cathedral? I don't want to take the wrong one. You seem to know the area well. I hope you don't mind me asking -- are you a local, or just visiting like me? It's a lovely city, isn't it? Anyway, thank you so much. It's so much nicer to ask a real person than to stare at a map.

For most of my life, I was the quiet one at the dinner table. I used to think you had to be funny or clever to be good at conversation. I was wrong. Over the years, I learned a simple trick: I stopped worrying about being interesting and started being interested. Now, when I meet someone new, I ask one gentle question, and then another. I let a pause sit instead of panicking. I try to read the room. And almost every time, that small bit of curiosity turns a stranger into a real conversation. You don't need the perfect words. You just need to be genuinely curious about the person in front of you.
